Name: joint_pruning_quantization_rate_distortion
Title: Joint Pruning and Quantization Under a Unified Rate-Distortion Framework
Short Hypothesis: Jointly optimizing pruning and quantization under a unified rate-distortion framework can achieve better compression efficiency than optimizing each technique independently, by leveraging their complementary effects on model redundancy.
Related Work: Existing work treats pruning and quantization separately under rate-distortion theory (e.g., Isik et al., 2021; Bu et al., 2019). While pruning reduces the number of parameters and quantization reduces their precision, their joint optimization has not been explored. This proposal distinguishes itself by unifying these techniques under a single framework, aiming to exploit their complementary effects on model redundancy.
Abstract: Model compression techniques, such as pruning and quantization, are typically optimized independently despite their complementary effects on model redundancy. This paper proposes a unified rate-distortion framework for jointly optimizing pruning and quantization, aiming to achieve better compression efficiency. By framing both techniques as lossy source coding operations, we derive a joint rate-distortion function that captures their interplay. We introduce a novel algorithm that iteratively prunes and quantizes the model, allocating bits to preserve task-relevant information while minimizing redundancy. Experiments on benchmark datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNet) demonstrate that joint optimization achieves superior compression ratios and accuracy compared to independently optimizing pruning and quantization. Our results highlight the benefits of treating pruning and quantization as complementary operations within a unified information-theoretic framework.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Compare joint optimization of pruning and quantization against independent optimization. Metrics: Compression ratio, accuracy, and task-relevant mutual information.
Experiment 2: Evaluate the impact of joint optimization on different model architectures (ResNet, ViT). Metrics: Compression ratio and accuracy.
Experiment 3: Analyze the rate-distortion trade-off achieved by joint optimization. Metrics: Rate-distortion curves, task-relevant distortion.
Experiment 4: Ablation study on the iterative pruning-quantization algorithm. Metrics: Compression ratio and accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk: Joint optimization may increase computational complexity during compression. Mitigation: Use efficient estimators for mutual information and task-relevant distortion.
Limitation: The framework assumes a fixed task-relevant distortion metric, which may not generalize across all tasks. Mitigation: Explore adaptive distortion metrics.
Risk: The iterative pruning-quantization algorithm may converge to suboptimal solutions. Mitigation: Use theoretical guarantees from rate-distortion theory to guide convergence.
